learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Loops, Two Pointers, Greedy Algorithms, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine two walls holding water between them. The amount of water depends on the shorter wall and the distance between them. To maximize water storage, you must intelligently choose which wall to move instead of checking every possible pair.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine two walls holding water between them. The amount of water depends on the shorter wall and the distance between them. To maximize water storage, you must intelligently choose which wall to move instead of checking every possible pair.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Opposite Direction Two Pointer pattern whenever the answer depends on two endpoints and you need to optimize a value involving both distance and height.
learning.signals: Whenever two endpoints determine an answer involving width or distance, consider using Opposite Direction Two Pointers.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are given an integer array height of length n. There are n vertical lines drawn such that the two endpoints of the ith line are (i, 0) and (i, height[i]). Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container that can hold the maximum amount of water. Return the maximum amount of water a container can store. You may not slant the container.

examples.explanation: The maximum area is formed by heights 8 and 7 with width 7. Area = 7 × 7 = 49.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Calculate the area for every possible pair of lines and return the maximum.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: The water level is always limited by the shorter wall. If we move the taller wall inward, the width decreases while the shorter wall remains unchanged, so the area can never increase. Therefore, always move the shorter wall.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Moving the taller line cannot improve the area because the shorter line still limits the water level.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever two endpoints determine an answer involving width or distance, consider using Opposite Direction Two Pointers.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Each pointer moves at most n times. Moving the taller line can never improve the answer because the shorter line still limits the water level while the width decreases.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever two endpoints determine an answer involving width or distance, consider using Opposite Direction Two Pointers.
commonWrongPaths: Using the taller height instead of the shorter height.
Using max(height[left], height[right]) instead of min().

hints: The area depends on width and the shorter height.
commonMisconceptions: Using the taller height instead of the shorter height.
Using max(height[left], height[right]) instead of min().

pattern.name: Opposite Direction Two Pointers
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever two endpoints determine an answer involving width or distance, consider using Opposite Direction Two Pointers.

approaches.title: Check Every Pair
approaches.idea: Calculate the area for every possible pair of lines and return the maximum.
algorithm: Pair it with every remaining line.

approaches.title: Two Pointers
approaches.idea: Start with pointers at both ends of the array. Compute the current area and move the shorter line inward because only a taller line can possibly increase the area.
algorithm: Move the pointer with the smaller height.
approaches.dryRun:
Left=1 Right=7 Area=8
Move shorter
Continue until pointers meet.

approaches.title: Opposite Direction Two Pointers
approaches.idea: Place one pointer at the beginning and another at the end. Compute the current container area. Since the shorter line limits the water level, move only the pointer pointing to the shorter line. Continue until both pointers meet.
approaches.intuition: Each pointer moves at most n times. Moving the taller line can never improve the answer because the shorter line still limits the water level while the width decreases.
algorithm: Move the pointer with the smaller height.
approaches.dryRun:
height=[1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7]
Area=(1,7)=8
Move left
Area=(8,7)=49
Move right
Continue...
Maximum=49

visualization.description: The shorter line determines the water level.

interviewFollowUps: Why does moving the shorter pointer always work?
Can this be solved using dynamic programming?
What if the container could be slanted?
Can you prove the greedy decision?
How does this differ from Trapping Rain Water?
interviewTips: State why the shorter wall limits the water level.
Justify moving only the shorter pointer.
Discuss the greedy proof if asked.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Moving the taller line cannot improve the area because the shorter line still limits the water level.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Two Ends → Compute Area → Move Smaller Height.

importantPoints: Water level depends on the shorter wall.
commonMistakes: Using the taller height instead of the shorter height.
Using max(height[left], height[right]) instead of min().

youtube: Take U Forward - Two Pointer Problems
Nick White - Container With Most Water
